summary: Fowler celebrates the DVD release of Sergio Leone's 'Once Upon a Time in the West,' arguing it's a masterpiece of slow, atmospheric filmmaking. He describes how the film's deliberate pacing, minimal dialogue, and visual storytelling create a hypnotic quality that rewards patient viewers. The opening ten-minute scene of gunmen waiting at a train station exemplifies the film's approach—drama through stillness rather than action. Fowler compares it favorably to Leone's other works, including 'The Good, the Bad and the Ugly,' noting how this film stretches a simple western plot into something operatic through its measured revelations and Morricone's pre-composed score.
key_points: The film's quality is evident in its opening: ten minutes of minimal dialogue and action that establishes its atmospheric, slow-paced approach
Leone tells stories visually through characteristic ultra close-ups rather than exposition, requiring viewers to read emotions in underplayed expressions
Morricone's score was composed before filming, creating an unusually tight fusion of music and visuals
The simple, predictable western plot is elevated by how Leone 'drips the story out drop by drop'
Despite its deliberate pace, the film maintains suspense and isn't difficult to follow
money_quotes: In that scene lies the slow pace of the whole film... But also in that scene you see the atmosphere and beauty of that film, which makes it one of my all time favorites.
The real dialog isn't spoken, it's seen in Leone's characteristic ultra close-ups.
People don't explain what they are thinking, you have to see it through their expressions, which are not exaggerated as in traditional drama, but underplayed.
I like a comment I've heard about this film—an opera where the arias aren't sung, they're stared.
The music and visuals are fused together with a memorable score that may not be Wagnerian in sound, but is certainly Wagnerian in its narrative intent.
It has a slow, hypnotic quality and I think there's a lot really going on.
key_insight: Great storytelling can emerge from restraint and visual understatement, where what remains unspoken and slowly revealed creates deeper engagement than conventional dramatic techniques.
